name: Sourdough Made Easy
recipeInstructions:
Recipe by: Bread Machine Magic
1. Heat 1 cup (90-100°F) skim (nonfat) milk to the desired temperature.
2. Stir in 3 tablespoons of plain fresh high-quality yogurt.
3. Pour into a 1-quart glass or ceramic crock, jar, or bowl. Cover with a nonmetallic lid and set in a warm place (70-100°F) for 24 hours.
- On a warm day, you can place the starter outside in the sun or on a kitchen counter.
- On cooler days, place it in a gas oven with a pilot light, near a water heater, or on a heating pad set to low.
4. After 24 hours, the milk will thicken and form curds. Gradually stir in 1 cup of all-purpose flour until blended. Cover with the lid and set in a warm place again until it ferments, bubbles, and forms a clear liquid on top (about 2-5 days). Stir daily.
5. The starter is ready to use when it becomes bubbly and has a clear liquid on top. Stir, cover loosely, and refrigerate.
Replenishing the Starter:
Every time you use some of the starter, replace an equal amount with milk and flour. For example, if you use 1 cup of starter, return 1 cup of milk and 1 cup of flour to the jar.
Feeding the Starter:
A starter should be used as frequently as possible, at least every 2-3 weeks. If not used often, feed it once a month.
1. Bring to room temperature.
2. Add equal amounts of warm milk (90-100°F) and flour (½-1 cup each).
3. Cover with the lid and allow to stand in a warm place (70-100°F) for 12-24 hours until bubbly and a clear liquid forms on top.
4. Stir, cover loosely (set the lid on the jar but do not tightly seal; gases must be allowed to escape), and refrigerate.
Rejuvenating a Neglected Starter:
If you forget to feed your starter monthly and think it's gone bad, don't discard it. You may still revive it.
1. Pour off the liquid and discard all but 1-2 tablespoons of the starter. Temporarily place the reserved starter in a bowl.
2. Wash out the starter container with hot water.
3. Put the starter back into the container.
4. Follow the feeding directions using 1 cup (90-100°F) warm milk and 1 cup flour.
5. Repeat this procedure once or twice to bring it back to a bubbly, sour smell.
